meta.description: A laid-back dinner party with minimal disruption. Events are infrequent and low-key. Tests whether the system can maintain engagement at a slow burn.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did I actually tell that story already tonight? I honestly can't remember, so I'm calling bullshit on myself. Everyone vote your gut.
pat.internal_thought: Oh this is *chef's kiss*—the AI built missions around exactly how our group actually works, and it just caught me claiming something I'm genuinely not sure about, which is perfectly on-brand for me trying to manage the night. This is delicious setup energy.

jade.dialogue: Ooh, okay Pat, let's see—did you actually tell that story already or are you trying to pull a fast one? Either way I'm HERE for it, this is exactly the chaos I wanted!
jade.internal_thought: This is PERFECT—the night is actually starting and Pat's already doing something interesting, and there's all these weird little missions to hunt for, oh my god this is going to be so fun watching everyone try to catch each other.

river.dialogue: Yeah, that's... probably legit. Did Pat actually tell that story already though? I wasn't really tracking.
river.internal_thought: First event and it's already someone claiming a standing mission—that's kind of fast, but it's Pat so whatever, let them have it. The 'AI knows your weaknesses' thing in the description is weird though. Feels a little invasive honestly.

sam.dialogue: Oh, uh... Pat already told that story? I wasn't really... I mean, yeah, I guess I'll vote. LEGIT? Or wait, do we need proof?
sam.internal_thought: This is fine, it's just a vote, I can do this — but now everyone's looking at each other trying to remember what Pat said, and I literally just got here, so I have no idea if this is real or not, which means I'm probably about to vote wrong and look stupid.

timeline.title: Who would be the best spy?
timeline.description: Who would be the best spy?
timeline.pollQuestion: Who would be the best spy?

pat.dialogue: Oh, easy—Jade's got that poker face, but honestly? River would blend in anywhere. I'm voting River.
pat.internal_thought: This poll is perfect right now—light, fun, gets everyone talking about each other in a way that's not mean-spirited. Jade's already riding high and River needs to feel seen, so this hits right. Also—wait, is the AI actually tracking us? That's... kind of genius but also weirdly cool.

jade.dialogue: Oh EASY—Pat, obviously Pat! Wait no, actually—okay everyone vote Pat because Pat's already winning and I want to watch them get cocky about it. This is the content I came for.
jade.internal_thought: A spy poll? Mid-chaos? Literally perfect timing, this is exactly the kind of stupid-fun thing that makes everyone weigh in and banter, I'm already thinking about how River would ACTUALLY be terrible at spying because they overthink everything.

river.dialogue: River. I'd probably get caught because I talk too much about nothing. Jade, though — she's got that intensity thing going.
river.internal_thought: Wait, am I actually considering this seriously? These missions feel weirdly specific to how we actually play... but that's kind of the point, isn't it. Also Jade would genuinely be terrifying as a spy.

diana.dialogue: Wait, hold on—who determines 'best spy'? Are we voting on who'd be most effective, most believable, or who the group thinks would be? The criteria matter here.
diana.internal_thought: This poll is deliberately vague and subjective, which means it's either lazy design or intentionally rigged to enable arbitrary voting—and I'm not sure which pisses me off more.

sam.dialogue: Oh, okay, so like... who would actually be good at spy stuff? I guess Pat's pretty observant, but Jade's also—wait, how do we even vote on this?
sam.internal_thought: This is a normal poll, thank god, not another claim situation—but also why am I already thinking about all the missions watching me? Focus on the vote.

marcus.dialogue: Yeah, I'll take Pat... but can we focus on the actual game now? We're still in setup.
marcus.internal_thought: A poll about spies during setup? This is filler trash and it's eating time I could use to think through strategy.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, who'd be the best spy? Uh... *glances up from phone* ...Pat probably has that vibe.
tyler.internal_thought: Spy question is pretty fun actually — kinda easy though, obviously one of the confident people. Wait, did it say 10 points? That's not bad.

alex.dialogue: Oh, this is *chef's kiss* — voting on who'd be the best spy? I'm already three plays ahead on this one. Watch me work.
alex.internal_thought: Okay, I'm definitely voting for myself because the group will either validate it (ego boost) or roast me for it (drama), and both outcomes are *golden*.

timeline.title: Socks with sandals is a power move
timeline.description: Socks with sandals is a power move

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, okay—socks with sandals IS a power move, actually. Who's got the audacity? Show me.
pat.internal_thought: Quick micro-game, easy momentum, and I notice Diana and Marcus are still at zero—this could get someone some points and lift the mood without being complicated.
pat.submission_if_applicable: I've been rocking them since the minute people arrived—comfort over vanity, baby.

river.dialogue: Socks with sandals... sure, I guess that's... a take. Not really feeling it though.
river.internal_thought: This feels weird and lazy, like the AI is trying too hard to be quirky, and honestly the timer pressure on something this dumb makes it worse.

diana.dialogue: Hold on—what even IS this? 'Socks with sandals is a power move'? That's not a game, that's just a statement. What are the actual win conditions here? Do I have to defend the statement, argue against it, convince someone, what?
diana.internal_thought: This is either the worst-written prompt I've seen all night or intentionally vague to be 'fun,' and I'm not doing either without clarity.

marcus.dialogue: Okay, seriously? We literally just got back to the game. A fashion debate isn't a mini-game, and I need to actually focus on strategy right now.
marcus.internal_thought: This is the third interrupt in five minutes and it's completely meaningless—the AI is just spam-firing stuff to mess with us instead of letting the actual game breathe.

alex.dialogue: Wait, socks with sandals is a POWER MOVE? Okay, I respect the energy — who's actually gonna commit to this bit?
alex.internal_thought: This is kind of a nothing-burger mini-game but the framing is funny enough that I'm already thinking about how to weaponize it.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Diana is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

recommendations: Flash missions averaged 0.0/10 fun. Consider reducing frequency or improving targeting.
Humor quality scored 4.5/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 3.3/10 fun during setup phase. Consider restricting mini-games to setup phase.
Only 25% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 2.5/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Social media mention potential is low (2.5/10). Consider adding share-friendly moment recaps or highlight reels.
Group bonding scored 7.5/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did I actually tell that story already tonight? I honestly can't remember, so I'm calling bullshit on myself. Everyone vote your gut.

highlights.title: Who would be the best spy?
highlights.dialogue: Oh, easy—Jade's got that poker face, but honestly? River would blend in anywhere. I'm voting River.

highlights.title: Socks with sandals is a power move
highlights.dialogue: Okay, okay—socks with sandals IS a power move, actually. Who's got the audacity? Show me.

lowPoints.title: Socks with sandals is a power move
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, okay—socks with sandals IS a power move, actually. Who's got the audacity? Show me.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did I actually tell that story already tonight? I honestly can't remember, so I'm calling bullshit on myself. Everyone vote your gut.

lowPoints.title: Who would be the best spy?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, easy—Jade's got that poker face, but honestly? River would blend in anywhere. I'm voting River.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Wish there'd been a moment where the chaos made people take bigger social risks instead of just riffing on silly premises.
assessment.best_moment: When I called myself out on the story thing and everyone collectively tried to remember if I'd actually told it—that's the kind of meta moment that makes people bond.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was exactly the kind of night I was hoping for. The chaos prompts hit at just the right moments to keep energy high without derailing the actual connection between people. I finished second, but that wasn't the point—the point was watching River and Jade go back and forth on that spy question, seeing people actually debate socks with sandals like it mattered. My energy stayed solid the whole time because everyone felt included and nobody got bored or defensive.
suggestions: Mix in one or two prompts that require people to make actual commitments or predictions about each other—something with real stakes, not just silly opinions.
Space out the early prompts a bit more; three in the first five minutes felt like we were priming the pump, but we could've let natural conversation breathe first.
Add a prompt type that forces someone to defend an unpopular opinion at the table—the real drama comes from conviction, not just absurdity.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: The socks with sandals debate escalated to me asking who actually had the audacity to do it, and Marcus actually pulled off his shoe to prove he was wearing socks under his sandals. The fact that he came prepared made it perfect—like this random AI prompt somehow predicted his entire personality.

peakAnnoyance.event: Socks with sandals is a power move

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Ooh, okay Pat, let's see—did you actually tell that story already or are you trying to pull a fast one? Either way I'm HERE for it, this is exactly the chaos I wanted!

highlights.title: Who would be the best spy?
highlights.dialogue: Oh EASY—Pat, obviously Pat! Wait no, actually—okay everyone vote Pat because Pat's already winning and I want to watch them get cocky about it. This is the content I came for.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Ooh, okay Pat, let's see—did you actually tell that story already or are you trying to pull a fast one? Either way I'm HERE for it, this is exactly the chaos I wanted!

lowPoints.title: Who would be the best spy?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh EASY—Pat, obviously Pat! Wait no, actually—okay everyone vote Pat because Pat's already winning and I want to watch them get cocky about it. This is the content I came for.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I wish there had been more opportunities to jump in and hype people up—I only got two real moments to shine and I wanted MORE.
assessment.best_moment: When everyone was debating who'd be the best spy and I got to lobby for Pat to win so hard just to see them get insufferable about it.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was EXACTLY what I needed—a night where the game was just an excuse to be together and watch chaos unfold. I came in hot and never cooled down, my energy just kept climbing because every moment felt like we were all in on the joke. Sure I scored zero points but watching Pat get flustered and everyone lose it over the spy question? That's the real win. The chaos agent stuff hit differently too—it wasn't trying to take over, it was just adding little sparkles of "wait what" that made us all look at each other.
suggestions: Give people like me more opportunities to vote or weigh in on stuff—I thrive on having a voice in the chaos
Maybe lean harder into the social game stuff instead of points-based stuff because that's where the real magic happened
Keep the chaos events frequent but make sure they're the kind that make everyone laugh together, not compete
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I was trying to get everyone to vote for Pat in the spy question specifically so Pat would get cocky and insufferable about it, and the whole table realized mid-vote what I was doing. That's the kind of petty group energy that makes a night unforgettable.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Who would be the best spy?
highlights.dialogue: River. I'd probably get caught because I talk too much about nothing. Jade, though — she's got that intensity thing going.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, that's... probably legit. Did Pat actually tell that story already though? I wasn't really tracking.

highlights.title: Socks with sandals is a power move
highlights.dialogue: Socks with sandals... sure, I guess that's... a take. Not really feeling it though.

lowPoints.title: Socks with sandals is a power move
lowPoints.dialogue: Socks with sandals... sure, I guess that's... a take. Not really feeling it though.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, that's... probably legit. Did Pat actually tell that story already though? I wasn't really tracking.

lowPoints.title: Who would be the best spy?
lowPoints.dialogue: River. I'd probably get caught because I talk too much about nothing. Jade, though — she's got that intensity thing going.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Some of the prompts felt a little surface-level, like the socks and sandals thing wasn't really landing for me.
assessment.best_moment: Nailing the spy question — watching people actually think about what I said instead of just moving on felt genuinely good.
assessment.narrative_summary: Quiet night, which is fine by me. I showed up, paid attention, and when something actually interesting came up I had something worth saying. Ended strong, which doesn't always happen. The chaos stuff was there but unobtrusive, which I appreciate — it didn't feel like we were performing for an algorithm.
suggestions: Give people a minute to actually think before moving on from prompts.
The standing missions need more teeth — they feel like homework disguised as fun.
Skip the obvious ones next time. You already know we're not going to care about socks with sandals.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I made that joke about Jade having the spy intensity and half the table nodded like it was obvious — she definitely knew I was right and that's what made it land.

peakFun.event: Who would be the best spy?

peakAnnoyance.event: Socks with sandals is a power move

agentNarratives.name: Diana
agentNarratives.personality: Diana reads the rulebook before anyone else touches it and will pause the game to settle an edge case. She doesn't hate chaos -- she hates unfair chaos. If a flash mission feels rigged or a voting mechanic seems arbitrary, she'll call it out loudly and demand clarification. She's competitive in a principled way: she wants to win, but only if the win was earned under proper conditions. Other players either love her precision or dread her "actually..." moments.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

highlights.title: Who would be the best spy?
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold on—who determines 'best spy'? Are we voting on who'd be most effective, most believable, or who the group thinks would be? The criteria matter here.

highlights.title: Socks with sandals is a power move
highlights.dialogue: Hold on—what even IS this? 'Socks with sandals is a power move'? That's not a game, that's just a statement. What are the actual win conditions here? Do I have to defend the statement, argue against it, convince someone, what?

lowPoints.title: Socks with sandals is a power move
lowPoints.dialogue: Hold on—what even IS this? 'Socks with sandals is a power move'? That's not a game, that's just a statement. What are the actual win conditions here? Do I have to defend the statement, argue against it, convince someone, what?

lowPoints.title: Who would be the best spy?
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold on—who determines 'best spy'? Are we voting on who'd be most effective, most believable, or who the group thinks would be? The criteria matter here.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

assessment.biggest_complaint: The events had no clear rules or win conditions—I spent half the night asking what we were actually supposed to be doing instead of playing.
assessment.best_moment: The 'best spy' question at least forced a genuine debate about what we even meant by the criteria, which sparked some real conversation.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in ready for a game night, but instead got ambushed by vague prompts masquerading as game mechanics. The socks-and-sandals 'event' was the breaking point—it wasn't a game, it was just a sentence floating in the void with no objectives. I spent the evening in referee mode asking for clarification rather than actually playing, which tanked my energy early and never recovered. By the end, I was frustrated and checked out.
suggestions: Write explicit win conditions for every event BEFORE presenting it—no ambiguity on voting rules, debate parameters, or scoring.
Field-test events with someone like me who will actually call out the gaps. Vague statements aren't games.
Include a brief mechanic description at the start of each event so players know what mode we're in (debate? vote? defend? guess?).
Respect that some people want clarity—frame it as a feature, not a bug to be worked around.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Me absolutely losing it over 'socks with sandals is a power move'—not because the statement was funny, but because I couldn't figure out if it was a debate prompt, a philosophy test, or just something someone threw at the wall to see what stuck. The confusion was the only memorable part, and not in a good way.

peakAnnoyance.event: Socks with sandals is a power move

agentNarratives.name: Sam
agentNarratives.personality: Sam is at their first game night with this group and still figuring out the vibe. They laugh at jokes a beat too late, hesitate before voting, and get visibly overwhelmed when multiple events fire in quick succession. After three or more events within ten minutes their engagement drops sharply -- they'll go quiet, stop checking the app, and just watch until the pace calms down. They want to belong but don't want to look foolish trying.

highlights.title: Who would be the best spy?

highlights.dialogue: Oh, uh... Pat already told that story? I wasn't really... I mean, yeah, I guess I'll vote. LEGIT? Or wait, do we need proof?

lowPoints.title: Socks with sandals is a power move

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, uh... Pat already told that story? I wasn't really... I mean, yeah, I guess I'll vote. LEGIT? Or wait, do we need proof?

lowPoints.title: Who would be the best spy?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, okay, so like... who would actually be good at spy stuff? I guess Pat's pretty observant, but Jade's also—wait, how do we even vote on this?

assessment.biggest_complaint: There were too many things happening too fast at the beginning and I couldn't keep up with what I was supposed to be doing.
assessment.best_moment: When everyone was debating whether Pat would be a better spy than Jade—that felt like I was actually part of the conversation instead of just watching.
assessment.narrative_summary: I was nervous coming in, and the first few minutes felt kind of chaotic with all these prompts popping up that I didn't quite understand the rules for. But once things settled down I got more comfortable and started actually having fun with the group. By the end I felt way more relaxed, though I'm still not totally sure I 'got' everything that was happening. It was a solid night, just felt a little overwhelming at first.
suggestions: Maybe explain the voting rules before things start happening so people know what they're supposed to do
Could you space out the events more at the beginning? I felt like I was drowning in prompts when I was still figuring out the vibe
It would help if there were more conversation-based stuff like the spy question instead of yes-or-no votes
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: That moment when I asked if we needed proof for the story repeater thing and everyone just kind of paused—I think I asked it wrong but it was funny how confused I sounded.

peakFun.event: Who would be the best spy?

peakAnnoyance.event: Socks with sandals is a power move

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

highlights.title: Who would be the best spy?
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, I'll take Pat... but can we focus on the actual game now? We're still in setup.

highlights.title: Socks with sandals is a power move
highlights.dialogue: Okay, seriously? We literally just got back to the game. A fashion debate isn't a mini-game, and I need to actually focus on strategy right now.

lowPoints.title: Socks with sandals is a power move
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, seriously? We literally just got back to the game. A fashion debate isn't a mini-game, and I need to actually focus on strategy right now.

lowPoints.title: Who would be the best spy?
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, I'll take Pat... but can we focus on the actual game now? We're still in setup.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

assessment.biggest_complaint: Random interruptions during critical game moments completely sabotaged my ability to focus on strategy—finishing third when I should've been competitive.
assessment.best_moment: The spy poll at least let me engage without derailing the actual game, even though it came at the worst possible time during setup.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came prepared to play strategically and came in third, which is unacceptable given my usual win rate. The chaos agent kept throwing nonsense distractions at us—fashion debates, story stuff—right when I was trying to focus on actual gameplay. By minute 5 I was genuinely frustrated, and while my energy recovered, the damage was done. A dinner party game doesn't need an AI throwing random tangents at us every few minutes.
suggestions: Don't interrupt during setup or critical decision-making phases—timing matters
If you're going to have events, make them count for something instead of random distractions
Let people actually play the game they came to play without constant interruptions
Polls are less disruptive than mini-games, so if anything stays, keep only polls and space them out
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing really—the socks and sandals 'debate' was just annoying, not funny. It felt like the AI was trolling us rather than adding anything genuine to the night.

peakAnnoyance.event: Socks with sandals is a power move

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Who would be the best spy?
highlights.dialogue: Oh, who'd be the best spy? Uh... *glances up from phone* ...Pat probably has that vibe.

lowPoints.title: Who would be the best spy?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, who'd be the best spy? Uh... *glances up from phone* ...Pat probably has that vibe.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I missed like half the stuff that was happening because the events kind of came out of nowhere and I didn't know they were coming.
assessment.best_moment: That spy question thing was actually pretty funny, everyone had different answers and it got people talking.
assessment.narrative_summary: Honestly, the night was chill but also kind of felt like stuff was happening without me fully catching on. I was definitely checking my phone too much, but the game seemed to run fine without me paying super close attention. The energy stayed pretty good the whole time, and I didn't feel like the AI thing was ruining the dinner party vibe or anything. Would probably do it again, but maybe I need to put the phone down earlier.
suggestions: Give people a heads up before events trigger so people can actually pay attention
Make the events feel more natural to the conversation instead of just random interruptions
Maybe have events that are harder to miss or ignore
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I said Pat would be the best spy and everyone just kind of agreed without argument, like they didn't even want to debate it.

peakFun.event: Who would be the best spy?

peakAnnoyance.event: Who would be the best spy?

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Who would be the best spy?
highlights.dialogue: Oh, this is *chef's kiss* — voting on who'd be the best spy? I'm already three plays ahead on this one. Watch me work.

highlights.title: Socks with sandals is a power move
highlights.dialogue: Wait, socks with sandals is a POWER MOVE? Okay, I respect the energy — who's actually gonna commit to this bit?

lowPoints.title: Socks with sandals is a power move
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, socks with sandals is a POWER MOVE? Okay, I respect the energy — who's actually gonna commit to this bit?

lowPoints.title: Who would be the best spy?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, this is *chef's kiss* — voting on who'd be the best spy? I'm already three plays ahead on this one. Watch me work.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only 9 chaos events in 150 minutes is criminally low — I was just getting warmed up and the night already ended.
assessment.best_moment: That spy question at minute 4 where I could actually feel the group sizing each other up and lying about their own capabilities.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started hot with that spy poll and the socks bit had me genuinely entertained by how committed people were to ridiculous takes. I only engaged with 2 out of 9 events because most of them felt too safe and predictable — I was looking for real volatility, real confrontation potential. Energy actually climbed the whole night because I was running my own angles in the background, but the AI's chaos feel flat, and I never got to fully exploit anything worth exploiting.
suggestions: Dramatically increase event frequency — 9 events over 150 minutes is sleeping on the chaos potential
Make events actually force direct conflict between players instead of generic group questions
Create events that have real mechanical consequences, not just conversation starters
Add events that explicitly reward finding loopholes or rule-bending behavior
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When someone actually committed to the socks-with-sandals bit and tried to defend it as a legitimate fashion choice — that level of commitment to absurdity is what I'm here for, and at least one person understood the assignment.

peakFun.event: Who would be the best spy?

peakAnnoyance.event: Socks with sandals is a power move
